articles.headline: Your Gym Staple Does a Lot More Than Build Muscle
articles.body: Creatine is known for power and strength, but scientists are now paying serious attention to what it does for your brain. A new review published this week confirms that creatine helps your body rapidly regenerate ATP, the fuel that powers both muscles and brain cells during intense activity. Studies show potential benefits for memory, mood, and processing speed, especially in people with lower natural creatine levels like older adults, women, and vegetarians. Researchers are also exploring its possible role in Parkinson's, depression, and menopause-related muscle and bone loss. Early findings are promising, but more research is still needed.
articles.takeaway: 3 to 5 grams of creatine monohydrate daily remains the most supported starting point for both physical and early cognitive benefits.

product.name: Thorne Creatine Monohydrate
product.blurb: The most researched supplement in history. Works for strength, brain health, and endurance.

articles.topic: women's hydration sports science research gap
articles.headline: Sports Science Has Been Guessing About Women's Hydration. That's Changing.
articles.body: Only 6% of global sports science research focuses exclusively on women. That stat is the driving force behind Gatorade's new multi-year initiative called Body of Science, launched this week with 500 women already enrolled in early studies. The Gatorade Sports Science Institute will now study women's hydration and nutrition needs across all life stages, including the menstrual cycle, pregnancy, and perimenopause. Decades of research has assumed women's physiology mirrors men's, leaving millions of active women without answers about what their bodies actually need to perform and recover.
articles.takeaway: Until female-specific hydration data catches up, active women should individualize fluid intake based on their cycle phase, sweat rate, and how they feel, not just standard male-based guidelines.

product.name: Skratch Labs Hydration Mix
product.blurb: Clean hydration without artificial junk. What Chris uses on long training days.

articles.topic: cortisol excess resistant hypertension MOMENTUM study
articles.headline: If Your Blood Pressure Won't Budge, Cortisol Might Be the Reason
articles.body: A major new U.S. study called MOMENTUM found that 27 percent of people with hard-to-treat high blood pressure, the kind that stays high on three or more medications, also have elevated cortisol levels. That is far higher than doctors had previously assumed. The study, presented at the American College of Cardiology's 2026 Scientific Session, screened over 1,000 patients across 50 centers nationwide. Excess cortisol is linked to weight gain, muscle loss, and diabetes on top of hypertension. Researchers say this discovery could open the door to new targeted treatments for people who haven't responded to standard blood pressure drugs.
articles.takeaway: If you or someone you know has blood pressure that won't respond to multiple medications, ask a doctor about screening for elevated cortisol as a potential underlying cause.

articles.headline: The Order You Use Your Recovery Tools Actually Matters
articles.body: A randomized controlled trial out of Shenyang Sport University tested six common recovery methods head to head for muscle soreness relief: massage, cold water immersion, vibration therapy, static stretching, electrical stimulation, and control. The finding that stands out isn't which tool won. It's that timing matters as much as method. Researchers found cold water immersion works best in the first 24 hours when inflammation peaks. Massage combined with electrical stimulation becomes more effective between 24 and 48 hours. Stretching plus massage is best for the 48 to 72 hour window when muscle function is being rebuilt.
articles.takeaway: Stop treating recovery as one-size-fits-all. Ice or cold water in the first day, then massage in the second, and stretching combined with massage on day three gives your muscles the right signal at the right time.
articles.pexelsQuery: ice bath cold water tub athlete

product.name: TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller
product.blurb: Five minutes of rolling saves hours of stiffness. Non-negotiable on hard training days.

quote.text: The mind is the limit. As long as the mind can envision the fact that you can do something, you can do it.
quote.author: Arnold Schwarzenegger
quote.context: Seven-time Mr. Olympia, actor, and former Governor of California, known for pushing the boundaries of physical and mental performance.

workout.title: Sweet Spot Surge Ride
equipment: Heart rate monitor or RPE scale
workout.warmup: Spin easy at low resistance for 8 minutes, gradually increasing cadence to around 85-90 RPM. In the last 2 minutes of warmup, add two 15-second pickups at a moderate pace to prime the legs.

exercises.name: Steady Base Ride
exercises.notes: Hold zone 2 effort, conversational pace, RPE 4-5 out of 10. Focus on smooth, circular pedal stroke.

exercises.name: Sweet Spot Intervals
exercises.notes: Push to RPE 7-8, just below all-out. This is the zone where you can speak a few words but not hold a conversation. Keep cadence at 80-90 RPM.

exercises.name: High Cadence Spin
exercises.notes: Drop resistance, spin at 100-110 RPM. Focus on smooth leg turnover without bouncing in the saddle. Rest 1 min easy between each.

exercises.name: Standing Climb Surge
exercises.notes: Increase resistance and stand out of the saddle, driving through the whole pedal stroke. Drop back to seated and easy spin for 2 minutes between surges.

exercises.name: Tempo Hold
exercises.notes: Settle into a sustained RPE 6-7 effort to finish the main set strong. Maintain steady breathing and stay relaxed in the shoulders and arms.

workout.cooldown: Spin easy at minimal resistance for 5 minutes, letting heart rate drop naturally. Finish with 2 minutes of standing quad, hip flexor, and calf stretches.

product.name: Garmin HRM-Pro Heart Rate Strap
product.blurb: Chest strap accuracy beats wrist sensors every time. Essential for zone 2 training.

meal.title: Spicy Chipotle Chicken and Black Bean Bowl
ingredients: 1/2 cup canned black beans, rinsed and drained
1/2 cup cooked brown rice or cauliflower rice
2 tbsp plain Greek yogurt (as sour cream swap)
meal.instructions: 1. Season chicken breast with chipotle powder, smoked paprika, garlic powder, salt, and pepper. Cook in a lightly oiled skillet over medium-high heat for 5-6 minutes per side until cooked through, then slice. 2. While chicken rests, warm black beans and corn in the same pan for 2 minutes. 3. Build the bowl: start with rice, then add beans, corn, diced tomatoes, and sliced chicken. 4. Top with Greek yogurt, avocado slices, a squeeze of lime, and fresh cilantro.
meal.tip: Grill or air-fry the chicken on Sunday and store sliced in the fridge. Bowls come together in under 5 minutes on busy weekdays.

product.name: Greater Goods Digital Food Scale
product.blurb: Measure your food for two weeks and you will understand your body better than any diet book.
